Bioinformatics Lecture 3:
• PSIBLAST – theory and practice!
Sept. 4, 2019
H. Goodson U. Notre Dame
Some slides this lecture modified from “BLAST Workshop” by Maya Schushan
1

https://biology.stackexchange.com/questions/11263/wh
at-is-the-difference-between-local-and-global-
sequence-alignments

How BLAST works
A nontechnical explanation…
Text modified from www.mssm.edu/library/reference/pdfs/BLAST%20Handout.pdf [page is no longer active, but I still have to cite it!]
BLAST does the following:
1)
It breaks a query sequence into a series of “words” of a set number of letters. 
Sequence: 
GESGAGKT
3-letter words:
GES, ESG, SGA  
word length can be adjusted in “algorithm parameters”, but this is rarely necessary
2) These words are compared to words from sequences in the database. 
3) When a match is found, the sequences are aligned locally by extending from this 
initial word match and scored using a “scoring matrix.”
aligned locally:  penalty for gaps; if gap is too big, regions are considered separately
4) “Hits” are presented to you in the order of the highest score/lowest E value
E value:  how likely the similarity between your sequence and the one in the database is due to chance

4
Example: Aligning Two Globins 
(HH)  VLSPADKTNVKAAWGKVGAHAGYEG
(SWM) VLSEGEWQLVLHVWAKVEADVAGHG
No Gaps:
• Percent identity: 36
• Percent similarity: 40
What Is An Alignment?
How does BLAST work?

5
(HH)  VLSPADKTNVKAAWGKVGAH-AGYEG
(SWM) VLSEGEWQLVLHVWAKVEADVAGH-G
Example: Aligning Two Globins 
With Gaps:
• Gaps: 2
• Percent identity: 45.833 (instead of 36 without gaps)
• Percent similarity: 54.167 (instead of 40 without gaps)
What Is An Alignment?
How does BLAST work?

6
• The final score of the alignment is the sum of the 
positive scores and penalty scores:
+ Number of Identities
+ Number of Similarities
- Number of Gap insertions
Number of Gap extensions
-
Alignment score
Scoring
Scoring 
Matrix
Gap 
penalties
What Is An Alignment?
How does BLAST work?

8
Possible to improve on BLAST?
Position-specific scoring matrix!  “PSSM”

9
PSI-BLAST
Step 1:
1.
Start with a standard protein-protein BLAST search 
that uses a standard substitution matrix  (e.g. BLOSUM62)
2.
Use the results to build a position specific scoring 
matrix (PSSM) according to alignment of all the hits 
with  low E-value.
Step 2:
1.
Perform a BLAST search using the PSSM to evaluate 
the pairwise alignments.  
➜PSSM vs. DB  instead of seq vs. DB  
2.
Update the PSSM according  to the new result
3.
Go back to the beginning of step two (iterate!) or stop.

10
BLOSUM62 Protein Scoring Matix
11/08/2016
10
R
K
K
P

Differences between BLAST and PSI-BLAST??
1)
BLAST scores are based on generic substitution matrix
Default= BLOSUM 62
2)
PSI-BLAST runs a regular blast search, then makes a multiple-sequence 
alignment (MSA) of all hits above a threshold, then  calculates a 
“position-specific scoring matrix”
“PSSM”
3)
PSI-BLAST uses this PSSM to conduct a more sensitive search of the 
database
4)
PSSM can be optimized by running multiple iterations
➥powerful but can be dangerous!
- Sometimes converges to a clear set of homologs
- Sometimes diverges to include clearly irrelevant sequences!
Advantages of PSI-BLAST:
• much more sensitive scoring system: each position has its own matrix!
• much better at finding distant relatives
Disadvantages of PSI-BLAST:
• If irrelevant sequences get included in the PSSM, PSI-BLAST returns to you a 
mixture of related and unrelated sequences!  
• Depending on the frequency of these unrelated sequences in the database, this 
can get worse each iteration!
12

Some practical “Rules of Thumb:”
• Sequences with >25% identity are usually homologous
longer aligned regions are more likely real!
• Sequences with < 25% identity are in the “twilight zone”
➥10% identity can be meaningful it is the “right” 10%!
➥some proteins can be aligned structurally but not by 1° sequence!
➤How do you tell whether “the right 10% are conserved?
• Regular BLAST E values of 10-4 are usually significant
• PSI-BLAST E values can be difficult to interpret 
➥if irrelevant sequences have been included, the results will be 
misleading
• BEWARE COILED-COIL!
• Searching for short sequences or low complexity sequences? 
➥usually require alterations to the default parameters. 
13

14
BLAST and PSI-BLAST
Lets sum up…
-
Blast is a fast way to find homologues
-
No analytic theory that estimates the statistical 
significance of gapped alignments
-
Gap scores have been selected by trial and error.
applying different scoring matrix è No 
guarantee for gap scores 
-
PSI-BLAST finds weak homologues fast

15
1.
A much more sensitive scoring system .
each position has its own pattern probabilities .
2.
More weight to conserved positions.
3.
Lowers the influence of random sequence noise.
➥Good at finding distant relatives.
The power of PSI-BLAST:

Assignment (before Monday)
•
Using PSI-BLAST, reproduce for yourself the yeast actin searches we did 
in class.  
More specifically, using the Model Organisms Database, show the following:
– With BLAST (or the first iteration of PSI-BLAST), you find only some ARPs
– With the  second iteration of PSI-BLAST, you find all 10 ARPs
– With additional iterations of PSI-BLAST,  you initially start finding bacterial 
relatives of actin (e.g., mreB), but as the number of iterations inceases, you 
start to get more distant superfamily members such as HSP70 and even 
hexokinase
•
Use PSI-BLAST to identify the set of relatives of your protein in the model 
organism database.  
–
Try different numbers of iterations – does the search converge or diverge? 
–
Be careful to record your search process so that you can explain and reproduce it.
•
Download the sequences, and rename them using the Perl program 
“Rename” (on your mac or that of a friend). Bring this to class Monday!
